card.intuition: TCP is a reliable, ordered handshake-based conversation; UDP is a fast, fire-and-forget message delivery.
card.analogy: TCP is like a recorded delivery letter that requires a signature; UDP is like regular mail or shout-broadcasting where you don't check if everyone heard you.
when_to_use: Use TCP for file transfers, emails, and web browsing where data integrity matters
Use UDP for live video streaming, online gaming, and VoIP where speed is more important than occasional lost packets
gotchas: UDP doesn't guarantee order — packets can arrive out of sequence
TCP congestion control can slow down your connection if it detects packet loss, even if you want speed

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
TCP: connection-oriented. SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK before any data.
TCP use cases: anything needing reliability and ordering.
UDP: connectionless. Fire and forget. Lower latency, no guarantees.
UDP use cases: real-time where fresh data beats old retransmit.

exercise.prompt: Complete the logic to select the transport protocol for different applications.
hints: User Datagram Protocol (UDP) is optimized for speed
exercise.test_code:
assert select_protocol("file_transfer") == "TCP", "File transfer needs reliability"
assert select_protocol("video_stream") == "UDP", "Real-time video needs speed"
print("PASS")
